task: We had a brief power outage at my place and the backup setup came back on, but I’m not fully convinced it behaved normally. What’s the smartest way to check the obvious failure points and decide whether I should keep using it or dig deeper before I trust it again?

turns.agent_response:
Smartest approach: treat it like a **real event log**, not a “seems fine” recovery.

### 1) Capture the exact sequence
Write down, in order:
- What failed first: utility power, inverter/UPS, generator, transfer switch, or load
- Did it **pull in**, **drop out**, **re-energize**, or **chatter/bounce**?
- Did any alarm **latch** after power returned?
- What did the indicator lights do vs. what the load actually did?

That sequence matters because it points to specific failure modes:
- **Brownout / undervoltage** → relay or controller partially drops
- **Contact bounce/chatter** → load flicker, repeated transfer, nuisance alarms
- **Welded/contaminated contacts** → indicator says one thing, load behaves differently
- **Delayed dropout from stored energy/flyback** → relay hangs on briefly after it should release
- **Bad debounce / input conditioning** → controller “lies” about state changes
- **Miswired NO/NC logic** → inverted alarm or false “normal” indication

### 2) Check the obvious physical failure points
If this is a generator / transfer system, inspect:
- **Battery terminals**: corrosion, looseness, heat marks
- **Oil cap / dipstick**: condition, evidence of recent service
- **Air intake / filter housing**: clogged, disturbed, or obviously untouched
- **Fuel condition**: stale smell, contamination, low level
- **Control panel**: any stored alarms, fault codes, odd resets
- **Enclosure / hour meter / covers**: signs someone opened it recently

Given your past experience with “maintained” equipment that still had junk batteries and sticking relays, I’d be skeptical until the hardware and the paperwork agree.

### 3) Verify recent service claims if someone says it was “maintained”
Check:
- **Service date vs. outage date**
- **Service hours vs. hour meter**
- **Who did it**: dealer, electrician, handyman, owner
- **What was actually serviced**: oil/filter, air filter, plugs, fuel system, battery, transfer switch
- Ask for **old parts or photos** if available

### 4) Do one negative control before trusting it
Don’t conclude “good” from one successful restart.

A better test is:
- Repeat under the **same conditions** if possible
- Compare behavior against a known normal case
- Look for the same sequence twice, not just one happy outcome

### 5) Decide: keep using vs. dig deeper
**Keep using for now** only if:
- No alarms
- No flicker/chatter
- Transfer/recovery was clean and repeatable
- Physical inspection looks normal
- No mismatch between indicator state and actual load behavior

**Dig deeper before trusting it** if:
- Any load flicker occurred
- Any alarm latched
- The relay/transfer action sounded abnormal
- Indicators and actual behavior disagreed
- Service history is vague or unsupported
- You saw corrosion, weak battery signs, or evidence of recent tampering

If you want, I can give you a **short on-site checklist** for the exact backup setup you have: **generator + ATS**, **UPS**, or **battery inverter**.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty much the right direction. I’d want it a little more concrete though — I don’t care about generic “looks normal” stuff.

Give me a tighter checklist by setup type. Generator + ATS is one thing, UPS/inverter is another. I want the actual failure points to check first, in order, and what would make me stop trusting it. Also tell me what readings or logs are worth saving so I’m not just guessing later.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, **failure-point-first** checklist by setup type, with **stop-trusting thresholds** and **what to log**.

## 1) Generator + ATS
Check in this order:

### A. Utility loss / start sequence
What to look for:
- Did the ATS **transfer cleanly** once, or did it **bounce/chatter**?
- Did the generator start on the **first crank** or need multiple tries?
- Did it reach stable running speed before load transfer?

Stop trusting it if:
- ATS chatters or transfers more than once
- Generator starts slowly, stalls, or needs repeated cranks
- Engine hunts, surges, or never settles

Log:
- Outage start time
- Time to generator start
- Time to transfer
- Number of start attempts
- Any audible chatter or flicker

### B. Transfer contact behavior
What to look for:
- Any flicker on loads during transfer
- Any relay/contactor buzzing
- Any smell of hot plastic or ozone
- Any fault light or transfer alarm after return

Stop trusting it if:
- Load flickered
- Contacts buzzed or arced
- Transfer felt delayed or unstable
- ATS reports a fault or fails to retransfer normally

Log:
- Whether the load stayed up
- Any flicker duration
- ATS status lights before/after
- Fault codes

### C. Generator electrical output
What to check:
- Output voltage on each leg
- Frequency
- Any undervoltage/overvoltage under load

Useful trip points:
- **Voltage** outside about **±5%** of nominal under steady load
- **Frequency** outside about **59–61 Hz** for a 60 Hz system
- Big voltage sag when loads start

Stop trusting it if:
- Voltage or frequency is unstable
- It only looks okay with light load
- Motor loads cause obvious sag or stumble

Log:
- Voltage L1-L2 / L1-N / L2-N if applicable
- Frequency
- Load level when measured
- Any sag when AC, pump, fridge, or other motor loads kicked on

### D. Battery / starting system
What to check:
- Battery voltage at rest
- Voltage during crank
- Corrosion/loose terminals
- Charge state from the charger

Useful trip points:
- Resting battery below about **12.4 V** on a 12 V system is worth attention
- Cranking voltage dropping below about **10 V** is suspicious
- Slow crank, clicking, or dim control panel = weak battery/connection

Stop trusting it if:
- It cranks weakly
- It fails one start attempt
- Battery terminals are corroded or loose
- Charger/maintainer isn’t keeping up

Log:
- Battery voltage at rest
- Voltage while cranking
- Start attempt count
- Any battery alarms

### E. Engine-side basics
What to check:
- Oil level
- Coolant level if liquid-cooled
- Fuel level/quality
- Air filter restriction
- Any stored engine fault codes

Stop trusting it if:
- Low oil, low coolant, fuel contamination, or fault codes are present
- It shut down on temperature/oil pressure
- It smells like raw fuel or overheated insulation

Log:
- Oil/coolant/fuel status
- Fault codes
- Shutdown reason if any

---

## 2) UPS / Inverter
Check in this order:

### A. Transfer / ride-through behavior
What to look for:
- Did the load stay up without a reset?
- Any brief drop, reboot, or flicker?
- Did it transfer to battery instantly and back cleanly?

Stop trusting it if:
- Equipment rebooted
- Lights flickered
- Transfer was delayed or repeated
- UPS/inverter alarmed during the event

Log:
- Input loss time
- Transfer to battery time
- Return-to-line time
- Any reboot/flicker

### B. Battery health
What to check:
- Battery age
- Battery voltage
- Self-test results
- Any swollen, hot, or leaking batteries

Useful trip points:
- Battery pack that fails self-test
- Any battery much weaker than the others
- Resting voltage low enough that runtime is clearly reduced
- Unexpectedly short runtime under load

Stop trusting it if:
- Self-test fails
- Runtime is way below spec
- Battery pack is hot, swollen, or mismatched
- One battery drags the string down

Log:
- Battery age
- Self-test result/code
- Runtime estimate
- Battery voltage if the unit reports it

### C. Inverter output under load
What to check:
- Output voltage
- Frequency
- Any overload or transfer fault
- Whether motor loads or power supplies cause instability

Stop trusting it if:
- Output sags under normal load
- It trips on a load that used to be fine
- Fans, pumps, or compressors make it hiccup
- It alarms on overload without a real overload

Log:
- Output voltage/frequency
- Load percentage
- Alarm history
- Which device was running when it failed

### D. Bypass / transfer path
What to check:
- If it has bypass, does it move to bypass cleanly?
- Any bypass fault or static switch fault?
- Does the load stay powered in bypass?

Stop trusting it if:
- Bypass fails
- Static switch faults
- It can’t return from bypass cleanly

Log:
- Bypass status
- Any transfer warnings/faults
- Event log entries

---

## 3) What to save every time
This is the part that helps later when you’re deciding whether it was a one-off or a pattern:

### Save:
- Exact **time/date**
- **Outage duration**
- **Sequence of events**
- Any **alarm codes**
- **Voltage/frequency** readings if available
- **Battery voltage** or UPS battery status
- **Load present** during the event
- Any **flicker, reboot, chatter, or delay**
- Photos of the control panel or app logs

### Best evidence:
- Event log export from the ATS/UPS/generator controller
- A phone photo of the display showing fault codes
- A short note like:
  - “Utility lost 8:14 PM”
  - “Generator started 8:16 PM, 2 attempts”
  - “ATS transferred at 8:17 PM, no flicker”
  - “Returned to utility at 8:31 PM, no alarms”

---

## 4) Simple decision rule
### Keep using it for now if:
- Start/transfer was clean
- No flicker or reboot
- No alarms
- Voltage/frequency stayed in range
- Battery/start system looks healthy
- Event log matches what you saw

### Dig deeper before trusting it if:
- Any flicker, chatter, reboot, or alarm happened
- Start was slow or inconsistent
- Battery looks weak or old
- Output was unstable under load
- Logs and real behavior don’t match

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page “buy / maybe / walk” checklist** for either:
1. **Generator + ATS**, or  
2. **UPS/inverter**  
with concrete pass/fail thresholds.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still a little too broad in spots.

I want the pass/fail thresholds tighter, and I want the order to be the order I’d actually inspect in the field — what I check first because it fails most often or tells me the most. Also split out:
- residential standby generator + ATS
- small inverter/UPS with battery bank

For each one, give me:
1) top 5 failure points in order
2) what measurement or log proves each point
3) what would make me stop trusting it immediately

And don’t hand-wave with “looks normal” or “stable.” I want the actual numbers or specific symptoms worth writing down.

turns.agent_response:
Got it. Here’s the tighter version in the order I’d actually inspect it in the field: **highest-probability / highest-signal first**, with **numbers, symptoms, and stop-trusting triggers**.

---

# 1) Residential standby generator + ATS

## Top 5 failure points, in order

### 1) ATS transfer contactor / switch behavior
This is the part that decides whether your loads actually stayed protected.

**What proves it**
- ATS event log showing:
  - utility loss
  - transfer to generator
  - retransfer to utility
- Physical status indication of **source position** if the ATS exposes it
- Audible transfer event: one clean change, not repeated clacking
- Load behavior: no reboot/flicker on sensitive loads

**Write down**
- Exact transfer time
- Whether it transferred **once** or **bounced/chattered**
- Any light flicker or device reboot
- Any ATS fault code
- Whether source indicator matched actual load behavior

**Stop trusting it immediately if**
- The ATS **chatters**, oscillates, or transfers more than once
- Any load drops, reboots, or visibly flickers during transfer
- The ATS log is missing the transfer event
- Source indication and actual load behavior disagree
- You can’t see ATS open/closed or exercise/fault history and the vendor won’t show it  
  - For a standby generator, that is not “weak proof” — that’s hiding the part that fails.

---

### 2) Generator start reliability
If it doesn’t start cleanly on demand, nothing else matters.

**What proves it**
- Controller log with:
  - start command time
  - crank attempt count
  - start success time
  - any shutdown/fault reason
- Audible start behavior

**Write down**
- Number of crank attempts
- Time from utility loss to engine running
- Time from engine running to ATS transfer
- Any stumble, stall, or restart
- Any “overcrank,” “fail to start,” or “low battery” code

**Stop trusting it immediately if**
- It needs **more than 1 start attempt**
- Crank time is clearly long or hesitant
- It starts, stalls, then restarts
- It reports **overcrank**, **fail to start**, or **battery charger fault**
- It only works after manual intervention

---

### 3) Battery / charger / starting circuit
This is the most common hidden failure point in standby units.

**What proves it**
- Battery voltage at rest
- Battery voltage during crank
- Charger output or controller charger status
- Terminal condition

**Numbers worth writing down**
- 12 V battery resting:
  - **12.6–12.8 V** = healthy charged range
  - **12.4 V or lower** = suspicious
  - **Below 12.2 V** = bad news before a start event
- Cranking voltage:
  - **Stays above ~10.0 V** = generally acceptable
  - **Drops below 10.0 V** = weak battery / bad connection likely
- If it’s a 24 V starting system, scale accordingly:
  - resting roughly **25.2–25.6 V**
  - cranking should not collapse below about **20 V**

**Write down**
- Resting battery voltage before the event
- Cranking voltage lowest point
- Battery age if known
- Corrosion, loose lugs, heat marks
- Charger fault or low-charge indication

**Stop trusting it immediately if**
- Cranking voltage falls below the threshold above
- Battery terminals are loose/corroded
- Charger is dead or faulted
- Battery is old enough that you don’t know its date and the unit already showed a start issue
- It only starts after a charger reset or manual jump

---

### 4) Generator output quality under actual load
A generator that starts but makes bad power is still a failure.

**What proves it**
- Measured output voltage and frequency at the generator or ATS output
- Controller readings if they are known-good
- Behavior when a motor load starts

**Numbers worth writing down**
- 120/240 V residential standby:
  - Voltage should usually stay within about **±5%** of nominal
  - So roughly **114–126 V** on 120 V legs
  - And **228–252 V** line-to-line
- Frequency:
  - **60 Hz nominal**
  - **59–61 Hz** is the practical “don’t worry yet” range
  - Outside that, especially under load, is suspicious

**Write down**
- L1-N, L2-N, and L1-L2 voltage if available
- Frequency
- Load level when measured
- Whether fridge, well pump, AC, or other motor load caused sag or hunting

**Stop trusting it immediately if**
- Voltage is outside the ranges above
- Frequency wanders or hunts
- Motor loads cause obvious dimming, sag, or engine stumble
- The controller says one thing and a meter says another

---

### 5) Engine-side protection and shutdown history
This tells you whether it survived by luck or by design.

**What proves it**
- Fault history / shutdown log
- Oil pressure / coolant temp / low fuel / overtemp codes
- Physical fluid levels

**Write down**
- Any shutdown reason
- Any latched fault code
- Oil level
- Coolant level if liquid-cooled
- Fuel level and fuel age if known

**Stop trusting it immediately if**
- It shut down on low oil pressure, overtemp, or low fuel
- It has a latched fault you can’t clear and explain
- Fuel smells stale/contaminated
- Oil is low enough to matter

---

## Minimum “trust it for now” bar for generator + ATS
I’d keep using it only if all of these are true:
- One clean start
- One clean transfer
- No chatter, flicker, or reboot
- Battery voltage and crank behavior are healthy
- Output voltage/frequency stayed in range
- Event log matches what you saw

If any one of those fails, I’d dig deeper before trusting it again.

---

# 2) Small inverter / UPS with battery bank

## Top 5 failure points, in order

### 1) Battery bank weakness
This is usually the first real problem in small UPS/inverter systems.

**What proves it**
- Battery self-test result
- Runtime estimate vs. expected
- Individual battery voltage if the system exposes it
- Battery age and temperature

**Numbers worth writing down**
- 12 V lead-acid battery resting:
  - **12.6–12.8 V** charged
  - **12.4 V or lower** = weak/suspect
- For a 24 V bank:
  - **25.2–25.6 V** charged
  - **24.8 V or lower** = suspicious
- For a 48 V bank:
  - **50.4–51.2 V** charged
  - **49.6 V or lower** = suspicious

**Write down**
- Battery age
- Self-test result
- Estimated runtime at the current load
- Any battery module fault code
- Whether any battery is hotter, swollen, or leaking

**Stop trusting it immediately if**
- Self-test fails
- Runtime is clearly below what the load should get
- One battery is dragging the string down
- Any battery is swollen, hot, leaking, or smells odd
- The unit reports battery replace soon, battery weak, or similar

---

### 2) Transfer / ride-through performance
If the load reboots, the system failed even if it came back.

**What proves it**
- Event log showing input loss and transfer to battery/inverter
- Device uptime / reboot evidence on the protected load
- Timestamped alarm history

**Write down**
- Exact outage time
- Whether the load stayed up or rebooted
- Any flicker, dip, or restart
- Transfer time if the UPS reports it
- Return-to-line time

**Stop trusting it immediately if**
- Any protected device reboots
- Lights flicker or dim enough to notice
- Transfer is delayed beyond what the load can tolerate
- The UPS/inverter logged a transfer fault or output drop

---

### 3) Inverter output quality under load
This is where some units pass “idle” but fail in real use.

**What proves it**
- Output voltage
- Output frequency
- Load percentage
- Alarm log during the event

**Numbers worth writing down**
- AC output:
  - Voltage should stay within about **±5%** of nominal
  - Frequency should stay near **60 Hz**, roughly **59–61 Hz**
- If the unit is a pure sine inverter, any complaint from power supplies, motors, or chargers is a clue

**Write down**
- Output voltage and frequency during battery operation
- Load percentage at the time
- Which appliance or circuit was active
- Any overload, low output, or waveform fault

**Stop trusting it immediately if**
- Output sags outside the range above
- It trips on a load that should be normal
- It alarms when a fridge, pump, or other motor starts
- Sensitive electronics reset even though the unit stayed on

---

### 4) Bypass / static transfer path, if present
This is a separate failure mode from battery/inverter failure.

**What proves it**
- Bypass status in the event log
- Static switch or bypass fault code
- Successful transfer to bypass and back

**Write down**
- Whether bypass engaged
- Any bypass fault
- Whether the load stayed powered during bypass transfer
- Any “transfer fail” or “static switch” alarm

**Stop trusting it immediately if**
- Bypass won’t engage
- Bypass causes a drop
- Static switch faults appear
- It can’t return from bypass cleanly

---

### 5) Charger / DC bus / internal fault history
A lot of UPS problems show up here before total failure.

**What proves it**
- Charger current/status
- DC bus voltage if available
- Internal fault log
- Temperature alarms

**Write down**
- Charger state
- Any overtemp or DC bus fault
- Fan failure
- Internal alarm codes
- Battery recharge time after the outage

**Stop trusting it immediately if**
- Charger is not restoring the battery bank normally
- Recharge takes much longer than usual
- DC bus or overtemp faults appear
- Fans are dead or the unit is thermal-throttling

---

## Minimum “trust it for now” bar for UPS / inverter
Keep using it only if:
- The load did not reboot
- Transfer to battery was clean
- Battery test did not fail
- Output stayed within voltage/frequency range
- No bypass, charger, or thermal faults appeared
- Event log matches what happened

---

# What to save every time
For either system, save these:

- Exact **date/time**
- **Outage start and end**
- **Sequence of events**
- **Alarm/fault codes**
- **Battery voltage** or battery status
- **Output voltage and frequency**
- **Start attempt count** for generator
- **Transfer count** for ATS/UPS
- Photos of:
  - display
  - event log
  - fault codes
  - battery terminals if relevant

If there’s an app or controller export, save the raw log. That’s better than memory every time.

---

# Fast decision rule
## Keep using it for now
Only if:
- No chatter
- No flicker
- No reboot
- No fault code
- No weak battery/start signs
- Logs match what you saw

## Stop trusting it and dig deeper
If any of these happened:
- multiple start attempts
- transfer bounce/chatter
- protected load rebooted
- battery voltage sagged hard
- output voltage/frequency was out of range
- ATS/UPS logs don’t match the event
- the vendor won’t show the ATS/open-close/fault history on a standby generator

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page field checklist** with blank lines for:
- readings
- timestamps
- pass/fail
- and a final **buy / maybe / walk** decision.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, closer. But I’d tighten it one more notch and keep the numbers tied to actual field checks, not just generic ranges.

What I still want:
- **Which meter reading goes with which failure point** — not just “voltage/frequency,” but where I’d take it first: battery posts, ATS line/load side, generator output, inverter output, etc.
- **What’s the first thing that usually fails in practice** for each setup, not just the first thing in theory.
- For the generator, I want the **ATS contact state / transfer evidence** up front, then start circuit, then output. On a lot of these, the transfer gear tells you more than the engine does.
- For the UPS/inverter, I’d want **battery bank + transfer event log** before output quality. Output numbers are nice, but if the thing reset the load once, that’s already a problem.

Also, I’d rather see the thresholds written as:
- “write down and watch”
- “suspicious”
- “stop trusting now”

That’s the kind of cut line I can use without second-guessing it later.

If you want to finish it properly, make it a field sheet: **measurement point → normal-ish range → bad sign → immediate stop condition**. That’s the useful version.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the **field-sheet version**, ordered by what usually fails first in practice and where the most useful reading comes from.

---

# 1) Residential standby generator + ATS

## What usually fails first in practice
1. **ATS transfer/contact behavior**
2. **Battery / charger / start circuit**
3. **Generator start reliability**
4. **Generator output under load**
5. **Engine protection / shutdown history**

---

## Field sheet

### 1) ATS transfer/contact behavior
**Measurement point**
- ATS **event log**
- ATS **source indicator**
- If accessible: **line side vs load side** voltage during transfer

**What to save**
- Utility loss time
- Transfer-to-generator time
- Retransfer time
- Whether transfer happened once or bounced
- Any chatter, clack, flicker, or relay buzz

**Write down and watch**
- One clean transfer
- Source indicator matches actual load state
- No visible flicker on lights or sensitive loads

**Suspicious**
- Slow transfer
- One brief bounce
- Uneven clacking
- Source indicator and actual load behavior don’t quite match

**Stop trusting now**
- ATS chatters or transfers more than once
- Any protected load reboots or visibly flickers
- Event log is missing the transfer/retransfer
- Transfer position is unclear or inconsistent

---

### 2) Battery / charger / start circuit
**Measurement point**
- **Battery posts**, not just cable ends
- Charger output or controller battery status
- Crank voltage at the battery posts

**What to save**
- Resting battery voltage
- Lowest voltage during crank
- Battery age if known
- Charger fault/status
- Corrosion/loose lugs/heat marks

**Write down and watch**
- 12 V battery:
  - **12.6–12.8 V** resting = okay
  - Crank stays **above ~10.0 V**
- 24 V battery system:
  - Resting roughly **25.2–25.6 V**
  - Crank stays **above ~20 V**

**Suspicious**
- 12 V resting at **12.4 V or lower**
- Crank dips below **10 V**
- Slow starter, weak click, dim controller
- Any corrosion or loose battery connection

**Stop trusting now**
- Battery won’t hold the resting range
- Crank voltage collapses below threshold
- Charger fault is present
- It only starts after a reset, jumper, or manual intervention

---

### 3) Generator start reliability
**Measurement point**
- Controller log
- Audible start behavior
- Start attempt count

**What to save**
- Number of crank attempts
- Time from utility loss to engine running
- Any stall, stumble, or restart
- Any “fail to start,” “overcrank,” or “low battery” code

**Write down and watch**
- Starts on the first attempt
- Settles quickly without hunting
- No abnormal crank delay

**Suspicious**
- More than one crank attempt
- Hesitant start
- Brief stall then restart
- Start time clearly longer than normal

**Stop trusting now**
- More than one failed/partial start
- Overcrank or fail-to-start code
- It only runs after manual help

---

### 4) Generator output under load
**Measurement point**
- **Generator output terminals**
- **ATS load side**
- Meter at a real load condition, not idle only

**What to save**
- L1-N, L2-N, and L1-L2 if applicable
- Frequency
- Load level when measured
- Any sag when motor loads start

**Write down and watch**
- 120 V legs roughly **114–126 V**
- 240 V line-to-line roughly **228–252 V**
- Frequency roughly **59–61 Hz**

**Suspicious**
- Voltage drifting outside that band
- Frequency wandering or hunting
- Noticeable dimming when fridge, pump, or AC starts

**Stop trusting now**
- Voltage or frequency is clearly out of range
- Motor load causes stumble or repeated sag
- Controller reading and meter reading disagree

---

### 5) Engine protection / shutdown history
**Measurement point**
- Controller fault log
- Oil level
- Coolant level if liquid-cooled
- Fuel condition

**What to save**
- Shutdown reason
- Latched faults
- Oil/coolant/fuel status

**Write down and watch**
- No shutdown codes
- Oil and coolant at correct level
- Fuel smells normal

**Suspicious**
- Old fuel
- Low oil
- Any unexplained fault history

**Stop trusting now**
- Low oil pressure, overtemp, or low-fuel shutdown
- Unclear fault that keeps returning

---

# 2) Small inverter / UPS with battery bank

## What usually fails first in practice
1. **Battery bank**
2. **Transfer / ride-through event**
3. **Transfer event log mismatch**
4. **Inverter output under load**
5. **Charger / DC bus / thermal fault**

---

## Field sheet

### 1) Battery bank
**Measurement point**
- **Battery posts**
- UPS battery status screen / event log
- Individual battery modules if available

**What to save**
- Battery age
- Self-test result
- Runtime estimate
- Resting battery voltage
- Any hot/swollen/leaking battery

**Write down and watch**
- 12 V battery: **12.6–12.8 V** charged
- 24 V bank: **25.2–25.6 V**
- 48 V bank: **50.4–51.2 V**
- Self-test passes
- Runtime is close to expected

**Suspicious**
- 12 V at **12.4 V or lower**
- 24 V at **24.8 V or lower**
- 48 V at **49.6 V or lower**
- Runtime feels short
- One battery is warmer than the rest

**Stop trusting now**
- Self-test fails
- One battery drags the bank down
- Any battery is swollen, hot, leaking, or smells off
- Runtime is obviously below what the load should get

---

### 2) Transfer / ride-through event
**Measurement point**
- UPS/inverter **event log**
- Protected load behavior
- Any reboot evidence on the load

**What to save**
- Input loss time
- Transfer-to-battery time
- Return-to-line time
- Any flicker, dip, or reboot

**Write down and watch**
- Load stays up
- No visible flicker
- No reboot
- Transfer event is logged cleanly

**Suspicious**
- Very brief dip
- Load almost rebooted
- Transfer happened but felt sloppy

**Stop trusting now**
- Protected device rebooted
- Any visible flicker or dropout
- Transfer fault or output drop appears in the log

---

### 3) Event log vs. real behavior
**Measurement point**
- UPS/inverter event history
- Device uptime / app history
- Any alarm timestamps

**What to save**
- Exact event sequence
- Alarm codes
- Whether the log matches what you saw

**Write down and watch**
- Log agrees with the outage
- One transfer, one recovery
- No hidden fault entries

**Suspicious**
- Log is incomplete
- Timing doesn’t line up
- App says “normal” but load hiccupped

**Stop trusting now**
- Logs and real behavior disagree
- Missing or obviously incomplete event history
- Repeated hidden alarms

---

### 4) Inverter output under load
**Measurement point**
- **Inverter output terminals**
- Output during battery operation
- Meter while a real load is running

**What to save**
- Output voltage
- Frequency
- Load percentage
- Which appliance was on

**Write down and watch**
- Voltage roughly within **±5%** of nominal
- Frequency around **59–61 Hz**
- Load runs without complaint

**Suspicious**
- Voltage sags under normal load
- Fans/pumps/computers make it hiccup
- Output sounds or behaves rough under load

**Stop trusting now**
- Load resets even once
- Output clearly sags or trips on a normal load
- It alarms on a load that should be fine

---

### 5) Charger / DC bus / thermal fault
**Measurement point**
- Charger status
- DC bus status if shown
- Internal fault log
- Fan / temperature alarms

**What to save**
- Recharge time after outage
- Charger state
- Any overtemp or bus fault
- Fan failures

**Write down and watch**
- Battery recharges normally
- Fans run
- No thermal or DC bus alarms

**Suspicious**
- Recharge is slower than usual
- Fan noise changes
- Warm cabinet, odd smell, or thermal warning

**Stop trusting now**
- Charger fault
- DC bus fault
- Overtemp alarm
- Fan failure or thermal throttling

---

# Simple cut line

## Keep using it for now
Only if:
- ATS transfer was clean, or UPS ride-through was clean
- Battery checks are good
- Logs match what happened
- No flicker, chatter, reboot, or fault code

## Stop trusting it now
If any of these happened:
- transfer bounce/chatter
- load reboot
- weak battery voltage
- failed self-test
- output sag on a normal load
- logs don’t match the event

---

If you want, I can compress this into a **single printable checklist** with three columns:

**measurement point | write down / watch | stop trusting now**

user_simulator.text:
Closer, yeah. But I still want the actual field-sheet formatting, not another half-outline.

Do it like this:

- measurement point
- write down / watch
- suspicious
- stop trusting now

And keep it to the stuff I’d actually probe first in the field.

For the generator side, I want ATS contact state / transfer evidence first, then battery/start circuit, then output at the ATS load side or generator terminals. Not the other way around.

For the UPS/inverter, battery bank and event log first. If the load rebooted once, that’s already a stop condition — no need to bury that after output quality.

Also, tighten the numbers to the actual check point:
- battery posts, not cable ends
- ATS line/load side, not just controller lights
- generator output terminals
- inverter output terminals

The ranges are fine as a starting point, but I want them attached to the measurement point and the failure point they prove.

One more thing: for each setup, tell me the first thing that usually fails in practice, not just the first thing you listed. That matters. On standby gensets it’s usually the transfer gear or battery/charger path, not some abstract engine problem. On UPS/inverter setups it’s battery bank and event behavior before anything else.

So yeah — finish it as a compact field sheet. No fluff.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the compact **field-sheet version** in the order you’d actually check it, with the meter point tied to the failure point.

---

# Residential standby generator + ATS
**What usually fails first in practice:**  
1) **ATS transfer/contact behavior**  
2) **Battery / charger / start circuit**  
3) **Generator start reliability**  
4) **Generator output**  
5) **Engine protection / fault history**

| measurement point | write down / watch | suspicious | stop trusting now |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ATS line side / load side, plus ATS event log** | Utility loss time, transfer-to-gen time, retransfer time, whether transfer happened once, any chatter/clack/buzz, any flicker or reboot on loads, source indication vs actual load state | One brief bounce, slow transfer, uneven clacking, source light doesn’t quite match load behavior | ATS chatters or transfers more than once; any protected load flickers/reboots; transfer evidence is missing; source/load state is inconsistent |
| **Battery posts** | Resting battery voltage, lowest crank voltage, battery age, charger status, corrosion/loose lugs | 12 V battery at **12.4 V or lower** resting; crank dips below **10.0 V**; slow crank; weak click; dim controller | Crank voltage collapses below threshold; charger fault present; battery/terminals are corroded or loose; starts only after reset/jump/manual help |
| **Generator controller log + audible start behavior** | Number of crank attempts, time from outage to engine running, any stall/stumble/restart, fail-to-start / overcrank / low-battery code | More than 1 crank attempt; hesitant start; brief stall then restart; start time clearly longer than normal | Overcrank or fail-to-start code; repeated start attempts; it only runs after manual intervention |
| **Generator output terminals or ATS load side** | L1-N, L2-N, L1-L2, frequency, load level when measured, sag when motor loads start | 120 V legs outside about **114–126 V**; 240 V line-to-line outside about **228–252 V**; frequency wandering outside about **59–61 Hz**; noticeable dimming on motor start | Voltage/frequency clearly out of range; motor load causes stumble or repeated sag; meter and controller disagree |
| **Controller fault log + oil/coolant/fuel check** | Shutdown reason, latched faults, oil level, coolant level if applicable, fuel condition | Low oil, stale fuel smell, unexplained fault history | Low oil pressure shutdown, overtemp shutdown, low-fuel shutdown, or returning fault you can’t explain |

---

# Small inverter / UPS with battery bank
**What usually fails first in practice:**  
1) **Battery bank**  
2) **Transfer / ride-through event**  
3) **Event log mismatch**  
4) **Inverter output under load**  
5) **Charger / DC bus / thermal fault**

| measurement point | write down / watch | suspicious | stop trusting now |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Battery posts + UPS battery status / event log** | Battery age, self-test result, runtime estimate, resting battery voltage, any hot/swollen/leaking battery | 12 V battery at **12.4 V or lower**; 24 V bank at **24.8 V or lower**; 48 V bank at **49.6 V or lower**; runtime feels short; one battery warmer than the rest | Self-test fails; one battery drags the bank down; any battery is swollen/hot/leaking/smells off; runtime is obviously below what the load should get |
| **UPS/inverter event log + protected load behavior** | Input loss time, transfer-to-battery time, return-to-line time, any flicker/dip/reboot | Very brief dip; load almost rebooted; transfer felt sloppy | Protected device rebooted even once; visible flicker/dropout; transfer fault or output drop appears in log |
| **UPS/inverter event history vs. what you saw** | Exact event sequence, alarm codes, whether log matches the outage | Log incomplete; timing doesn’t line up; app says normal but load hiccupped | Logs and real behavior disagree; missing or obviously incomplete event history; repeated hidden alarms |
| **Inverter output terminals** | Output voltage, frequency, load percentage, which appliance/circuit was on | Voltage outside about **±5%** of nominal; frequency outside about **59–61 Hz**; fans/pumps/computers make it hiccup | Load resets even once; output clearly sags or trips on a normal load; it alarms on a load that should be fine |
| **Charger / DC bus / thermal status** | Recharge time after outage, charger state, DC bus status if shown, overtemp/fan faults | Recharge is slower than usual; fan noise changes; warm cabinet; odd smell; thermal warning | Charger fault; DC bus fault; overtemp alarm; fan failure or thermal throttling |

---

## Quick cut line

### Keep using it for now
Only if:
- ATS transfer was clean, or UPS ride-through was clean
- Battery checks are good
- Logs match what happened
- No flicker, chatter, reboot, or fault code

### Stop trusting it now
If any of these happened:
- transfer bounce/chatter
- load reboot
- weak battery voltage
- failed self-test
- output sag on a normal load
- logs don’t match the event

If you want, I can turn this into a **single-page printable sheet** with blank lines for readings and pass/fail boxes.

I’d print that as-is and use it.
